meta.title: DBX-MLP-2: Model Deployment, Serving and A/B Testing

questions.stem: A data scientist wants to log a trained scikit-learn model as an artifact in an active MLflow run so it can later be loaded for inference. Which function call correctly logs the model?

options.text: mlflow.log_artifact(model, 'sklearn-model')
options.explanation: Mlflow.log_artifact() logs arbitrary files by path — it expects a file on disk, not a Python model object, and does not produce a proper MLflow model directory with MLmodel metadata.

options.text: mlflow.log_param('model', model)
options.explanation: Mlflow.log_param() stores key-value string metadata — it cannot serialize or store a model object.

options.text: mlflow.sklearn.log_model(model, artifact_path='sklearn-model')
options.explanation: Mlflow.sklearn.log_model() serializes a scikit-learn estimator and logs it to the active run with full MLflow model metadata (MLmodel file, conda environment, model signature if provided), enabling later loading via mlflow.pyfunc.load_model().

options.text: mlflow.save_model(model, 'sklearn-model')
options.explanation: Mlflow.save_model() saves to a local path but does not log anything to the active run's artifact store.

questions.stem: Which statement correctly describes the relationship between MLflow experiments and runs?

options.text: A run contains multiple experiments, each with different hyperparameters
options.explanation: The hierarchy is inverted — experiments contain runs, not the other way around.

options.text: Each experiment can only contain one active run at a time
options.explanation: An experiment can have many concurrent active runs, which is exactly what SparkTrials exploits for parallel hyperparameter search.

options.text: Experiments and runs are the same concept with different names in different MLflow versions
options.explanation: Experiments and runs are distinct concepts at different levels of the hierarchy, not synonyms.

options.text: An experiment is a named container that groups multiple runs, where each run records parameters, metrics, and artifacts
options.explanation: In MLflow's hierarchy, an experiment is the top-level grouping entity (like a project or model family), and each experiment contains many runs. Each run is one execution of training code that logs its own parameters, metrics, and artifacts.

questions.stem: A data scientist enables mlflow.autolog() before training an XGBoost model. After training completes, they want to additionally log a custom validation metric computed on a held-out test set. What is the correct approach?

options.text: Call mlflow.autolog(disable=True) and then mlflow.log_metric() to replace all autologged metrics
options.explanation: Calling mlflow.autolog(disable=True) would disable future autologging but does not allow adding metrics to the existing run — it would end the autolog-started run context.

options.text: Create a new separate run to log the custom metric so autologged and manual metrics are not mixed
options.explanation: Creating a separate run for the custom metric breaks traceability — the custom validation metric and autologged training metrics would be in different runs with no connection.

options.text: Within the same active run context (or by passing the run_id), call mlflow.log_metric('test_custom_metric', value) after training
options.explanation: Autologging and manual logging are not mutually exclusive. Within the same run context opened by autolog (or by explicitly referencing the run_id using mlflow.start_run(run_id=...)), you can call mlflow.log_metric() to add custom metrics alongside autologged ones.

options.text: Custom metrics cannot be added to a run that used autologging
options.explanation: Manual logging is always available regardless of autolog status — they coexist within the same run.

questions.stem: An ML engineer constructs a HyperOpt search space that includes both a continuous learning rate and a categorical choice of optimizer. Which code correctly defines this combined search space?

options.text: space = {'lr': hp.uniform('lr', 1e-4, 1e-1), 'optimizer': hp.choice('optimizer', ['adam', 'sgd', 'rmsprop'])}
options.explanation: Hp.uniform() is the appropriate distribution for a continuous float like learning rate, and hp.choice() is correct for a discrete categorical variable like optimizer name. The dict structure with named keys is the standard HyperOpt search space format.

options.text: space = {'lr': hp.randint('lr', 100), 'optimizer': hp.uniform('optimizer', 0, 2)}
options.explanation: Hp.randint for a float produces integers, not the continuous range needed for learning rate, and hp.uniform for a categorical optimizer expects numeric bounds, not string lists.

options.text: space = {'lr': hp.choice('lr', [1e-4, 1e-1]), 'optimizer': hp.uniform('optimizer', ['adam', 'sgd'])}
options.explanation: Hp.choice for learning rate limits it to exactly two discrete values instead of a continuous range, and hp.uniform for optimizer expects numeric bounds rather than a string list.

options.text: space = [hp.uniform(1e-4, 1e-1), hp.choice(['adam', 'sgd', 'rmsprop'])]
options.explanation: Using a list format without named keys means HyperOpt cannot unpack the parameters by name when calling the objective function.

questions.stem: A data scientist uses mlflow.models.infer_signature() to create a model signature. Which inputs are required by infer_signature()?

options.text: A list of feature names and their data types as strings
options.explanation: `infer_signature()` derives schemas from actual data samples, not from manually typed field definitions.

options.text: A sample of the model input data and a sample of the model output (predictions)
options.explanation: `mlflow.models.infer_signature(model_input, model_output)` takes a representative sample of the model input data and a representative sample of the model output (predictions) to infer both the input and output schemas.

options.text: The trained model object and the training DataFrame
options.explanation: `infer_signature()` does not take the trained model object itself; it takes example inputs and outputs.

options.text: The MLflow run ID and the artifact path of the logged model
options.explanation: `infer_signature()` operates on example data, not on MLflow run metadata or artifact paths.

questions.stem: An ML team runs parallel hyperparameter trials using SparkTrials with HyperOpt. Select TWO behaviors that are true of SparkTrials compared to the standard Trials object. (Select TWO)

options.text: SparkTrials distributes each trial as a separate Spark task, allowing multiple trials to run concurrently on cluster workers
options.explanation: `SparkTrials` dispatches each HyperOpt trial as a Spark task to worker nodes, enabling parallel hyperparameter search across the cluster instead of running trials only on the driver.

options.text: When Databricks automated MLflow tracking is enabled and `fmin()` is wrapped in `mlflow.start_run()`, trial runs can be logged to the experiment automatically
options.explanation: Databricks documents automated MLflow tracking for `SparkTrials`: when you wrap `fmin()` in `mlflow.start_run()`, the experiment can automatically capture the tuning runs for comparison in the MLflow UI.

options.text: SparkTrials enables GPU allocation per trial using the parallelism parameter
options.explanation: The `parallelism` parameter controls how many trials run concurrently; it does not assign GPUs by itself. GPU usage depends on the cluster and workload configuration.

options.text: SparkTrials requires the objective function to return a dict with 'loss' and 'status' keys, whereas Trials only requires a scalar loss
options.explanation: Both `Trials` and `SparkTrials` use the same HyperOpt objective-function return pattern, typically a dict containing keys such as `loss` and `status`.

questions.stem:
A data scientist runs the following code but finds the feature importance figure is NOT visible in the MLflow UI artifacts tab. What is the issue?

```python
with mlflow.start_run():
    model.fit(X_train, y_train)
    fig, ax = plt.subplots()
    plot_importance(model, ax=ax)
    mlflow.log_artifact('/tmp/importance.png')
```

options.text: plot_importance() does not work with MLflow
options.explanation: Plot_importance() from XGBoost works with standard matplotlib figures and has no MLflow incompatibility.

options.text: mlflow.log_artifact() only works with text files, not PNG images
options.explanation: Mlflow.log_artifact() supports any file type, including images, binary files, and HTML.

options.text: The figure was never saved to '/tmp/importance.png' before calling log_artifact()
options.explanation: Mlflow.log_artifact() takes a file path and uploads the file at that path to the artifact store. The code never calls fig.savefig('/tmp/importance.png') before log_artifact, so the file does not exist at that path. The fix is to add fig.savefig('/tmp/importance.png') before log_artifact, or use mlflow.log_figure(fig, 'importance.png').

options.text: The artifact must be logged before model.fit() completes
options.explanation: Artifacts can be logged at any point within the run context — before or after training completes.

questions.stem: A data scientist compares two MLflow runs and notices that Run A was logged with mlflow.xgboost.autolog() while Run B was logged manually. Which metric will appear in Run A's logged metrics that would NOT typically appear in Run B unless explicitly added?

options.text: validation_0-rmse logged at each boosting round (step-wise metric)
options.explanation: Mlflow.xgboost.autolog() logs evaluation metrics (like validation_0-rmse, validation_0-logloss) at every boosting round as a step-wise time series, which would require manual calls to mlflow.log_metric('rmse', value, step=i) in a custom callback to replicate in Run B.

options.text: Total training wall-clock time
options.explanation: Total training wall-clock time can be logged manually and is not a unique feature of XGBoost autolog output.

options.text: Python interpreter version
options.explanation: Python interpreter version is captured in the conda environment file artifact, not as a tracked metric.

options.text: Memory usage per tree
options.explanation: Memory usage per tree is not logged by XGBoost autolog — it is not a standard autolog output metric.

questions.stem: A data scientist wants to store the path to the training dataset used in an experiment as metadata on the run for reproducibility. Which MLflow function is most appropriate?

options.text: mlflow.log_metric('dataset_path', '/mnt/data/train.parquet')
options.explanation: Mlflow.log_metric() only accepts numeric values — passing a string path would fail with a type error.

options.text: mlflow.set_tag('dataset_path', '/mnt/data/train.parquet')
options.explanation: Mlflow.set_tag() works technically but tags are for organizational/informational metadata, not reproducibility configuration parameters. The params table is the canonical place for run inputs.

options.text: mlflow.log_artifact('/mnt/data/train.parquet')
options.explanation: Mlflow.log_artifact() would copy the entire dataset file into the artifact store, which is expensive and unnecessary when storing just the path reference is sufficient.

options.text: mlflow.log_param('dataset_path', '/mnt/data/train.parquet')
options.explanation: Mlflow.log_param() is designed for logging configuration values that define the training run setup — including data paths, feature names, preprocessing flags, and hyperparameters. These appear in the run's params table and can be used to filter runs.

questions.stem: A model is currently in the 'Staging' stage of the MLflow Model Registry. After successful validation, an ML engineer wants to promote it to 'Production'. Using MlflowClient, which method performs this stage transition?

options.text: MlflowClient().update_model_version('MyModel', version=2, description='Promoted')
options.explanation: MlflowClient().update_model_version() updates the description field of a version but cannot change its stage.

options.text: MlflowClient().transition_model_version_stage('MyModel', version=2, stage='Production')
options.explanation: MlflowClient().transition_model_version_stage() is the dedicated method for moving a model version through the registry lifecycle stages (None → Staging → Production → Archived).

options.text: mlflow.register_model('models:/MyModel/Staging', 'MyModel')
options.explanation: Mlflow.register_model() from a stage URI would create a new model version rather than transitioning an existing version to a different stage.

options.text: MlflowClient().set_model_version_tag('MyModel', 2, 'stage', 'Production')
options.explanation: MlflowClient().set_model_version_tag() sets a metadata tag key-value pair but has no effect on the official stage — the version would still be in Staging.

questions.stem: A data scientist logs a model with a custom PythonModel class that wraps a preprocessing pipeline and a CatBoost classifier. The class implements the predict() method. Which MLflow call correctly logs this custom model?

options.text: mlflow.catboost.log_model(model, 'model')
options.explanation: Mlflow.catboost.log_model() requires the mlflow-catboost plugin and only handles native CatBoost models — it does not wrap custom classes with preprocessing pipelines.

options.text: mlflow.sklearn.log_model(model, 'model')
options.explanation: Mlflow.sklearn.log_model() only serializes sklearn-compatible objects using pickle/joblib and does not support custom predict() logic or preprocessing wrappers.

options.text: mlflow.pyfunc.log_model('model', python_model=MyCustomModel(), artifacts={'catboost_model': model_path})
options.explanation: Mlflow.pyfunc.log_model() with a python_model argument is the correct way to log a custom PythonModel subclass. The artifacts dict maps logical names to file paths so the predict() method can access them at inference time via context.artifacts.

options.text: mlflow.log_artifact(model, 'model')
options.explanation: Mlflow.log_artifact() expects a file path to upload, not a Python class object — passing a class instance would fail with a serialization error.

questions.stem: An ML team wants to ensure that any client application loading their model always gets the latest 'Production' version without needing to update model version numbers in client code. Which URI pattern achieves this?

options.text: models:/MyModel/3 (hardcoding the current production version number)
options.explanation: Hardcoding the version number means every client application must be updated whenever a new version is promoted to production — a maintenance burden.

options.text: runs:/run_id/artifacts/my_model
options.explanation: Runs:/run_id/artifacts/my_model is tied to a specific training run artifact, not a promoted registry version with stage semantics.

options.text: models:/MyModel/Production (classic registry stage-based URI)
options.explanation: The stage-based URI models:/ModelName/Production always resolves to whichever version is currently in the Production stage, making client code immune to version number changes when a new version is promoted.

options.text: file:///mlflow/models/MyModel
options.explanation: File:///mlflow/models/MyModel is an invalid URI format for the MLflow registry and would not resolve to a registered model.

questions.stem: A Feature Store team is designing feature tables for a recommendation system. Select TWO properties that must be defined when calling FeatureStoreClient.create_table(). (Select TWO)

options.text: name — the full three-part table name (catalog.schema.table)
options.explanation: The table name (fully qualified as catalog.schema.table) is a required parameter for FeatureStoreClient.create_table().

options.text: primary_keys — the column(s) that uniquely identify each entity
options.explanation: Primary_keys is required — it defines the entity key(s) used to look up features during training set creation and batch scoring. Without primary_keys, the Feature Store cannot perform joins.

options.text: partition_by — the column to partition the feature table storage by
options.explanation: Partition_by is optional and used for storage optimization, not required for table creation.

options.text: schema — the DataFrame schema defining feature column names and types
options.explanation: While schema is needed, it can be inferred from a provided DataFrame via the df parameter or specified via schema — it is not listed among the core required parameters alongside name and primary_keys.

questions.stem: A data scientist writes a feature pipeline that calls fs.write_table() with mode='merge'. What is the behavior of 'merge' mode in the Databricks Feature Store?

options.text: Overwrites the entire feature table with the new DataFrame
options.explanation: Overwriting the entire feature table is the behavior of mode='overwrite', not mode='merge'.

options.text: Appends all rows from the new DataFrame without deduplication
options.explanation: Appending all rows without deduplication is the behavior of mode='append', which can create duplicate primary keys.

options.text: Upserts rows — inserting new entity keys and updating existing entity keys with new feature values
options.explanation: Mode='merge' in FeatureStoreClient.write_table() performs an upsert: rows with primary keys that already exist in the feature table are updated with new values, and rows with new primary keys are inserted.

options.text: Creates a new version of the feature table without modifying the existing data
options.explanation: Feature tables do not have a versioning system in the write_table() interface — there is no mode that creates a new version without modifying existing data.

questions.stem: An ML engineer needs to find all runs in an experiment where val_f1 > 0.85 and the regularization parameter alpha is less than 0.01. Using MlflowClient, which filter_string should they use?

options.text: "metrics.val_f1 > 0.85 OR params.alpha < 0.01"
options.explanation: It uses OR instead of AND, which would match runs satisfying either condition, not both.

options.text: "metrics.val_f1 > 0.85 AND params.alpha < '0.01'"
options.explanation: Wrapping the param value in quotes ('0.01') makes it a string comparison, which breaks numeric ordering for params stored as strings in MLflow.

options.text: "val_f1 > 0.85 AND alpha < 0.01"
options.explanation: It omits the required metrics. and params. namespace prefixes — bare field names like val_f1 and alpha are not valid filter expressions in MLflow search syntax.

options.text: "metrics.val_f1 > 0.85 AND params.alpha < 0.01"
options.explanation: MLflow search filter strings require the namespace prefix — metrics. for metric values and params. for parameter values — and AND combines conditions correctly.

questions.stem: An ML engineer wants to add a human-readable description to a registered model version explaining the changes made. Which MlflowClient call accomplishes this?

options.text: MlflowClient().set_model_version_tag('MyModel', 2, 'description', 'Improved recall')
options.explanation: MlflowClient().set_model_version_tag() adds a tag key-value pair to the version's metadata, which is searchable but is not the same as the official description field.

options.text: mlflow.set_tag('model_description', 'Improved recall with new training data')
options.explanation: Mlflow.set_tag() sets a tag on the active training run, not on a registered model version in the registry.

options.text: MlflowClient().transition_model_version_stage('MyModel', 2, stage='Production', archive_existing_versions=True)
options.explanation: MlflowClient().transition_model_version_stage() changes the stage of a model version, not its description.

options.text: MlflowClient().update_model_version('MyModel', version=2, description='Improved recall with new training data')
options.explanation: MlflowClient().update_model_version() is the dedicated method for updating the description field on a specific model version in the registry. The description appears in the Model Registry UI.

questions.stem: A data scientist logs a model with mlflow.sklearn.log_model() but then discovers the model was logged without a model signature. Without retraining, how can they add a signature to the existing registered model version?

options.text: Call mlflow.register_model() again with a signature argument to overwrite the version
options.explanation: Mlflow.register_model() would create a new model version rather than updating the signature of the existing version.

options.text: Download the model artifacts, add the signature using mlflow.models.set_signature(), then re-upload the artifacts
options.explanation: MLflow provides mlflow.models.set_signature() which modifies the signature of an existing logged model in-place in the artifact store, without requiring retraining.

options.text: Signatures can only be added at training time — the model must be retrained
options.explanation: Mlflow.models.set_signature() exists precisely for this post-hoc scenario — signatures can be added without retraining.

options.text: Set a tag on the model version with key 'signature' containing the JSON schema
options.explanation: Tags are metadata stored externally and are not read by MLflow serving infrastructure as a model signature.

questions.stem: A Feature Store table stores daily aggregated user behavior features. An ML team wants to create a training set that joins these features to label events using point-in-time lookups. What additional configuration is required in the FeatureLookup definition?

options.text: Set output_name to specify where to write the joined features
options.explanation: Output_name renames the feature column in the output DataFrame, which has nothing to do with enabling point-in-time correctness.

options.text: Set mode='point_in_time' in create_training_set()
options.explanation: Mode='point_in_time' is not a valid parameter for create_training_set() — point-in-time behavior is controlled via timestamp_lookup_key in FeatureLookup.

options.text: Provide a join_type='left' argument to handle missing feature values
options.explanation: Join_type is not a parameter of FeatureLookup — the join behavior is determined by the feature table's timestamp key configuration.

options.text: Specify timestamp_lookup_key in the FeatureLookup to indicate which column in the labels DataFrame holds the event timestamp
options.explanation: To enable point-in-time lookups when calling create_training_set(), each FeatureLookup must specify a timestamp_lookup_key — the column in the observation/labels DataFrame that holds the event timestamp.

questions.stem: A data scientist creates a Spark UDF from an MLflow model for batch inference. What is the main difference between using env_manager='conda' versus env_manager='local' in mlflow.pyfunc.spark_udf()?

options.text: env_manager='conda' creates a conda environment matching the model's dependencies on each worker, ensuring reproducibility; env_manager='local' uses the existing Python environment on workers
options.explanation: Env_manager='conda' instructs each worker to create a conda environment from the model's conda.yaml, ensuring the exact library versions used during training are present at inference time. env_manager='local' uses whatever Python environment is already installed on the workers, which is faster to start but risks version mismatches.

options.text: env_manager='conda' runs inference on the driver only; env_manager='local' uses all workers
options.explanation: Both options distribute inference across workers.

options.text: env_manager='conda' only works with sklearn models; env_manager='local' works with all flavors
options.explanation: Both work with all model flavors.

options.text: env_manager='conda' is required for GPU models; env_manager='local' is for CPU models
options.explanation: GPU/CPU configuration is unrelated to the env_manager choice.

questions.stem: An ML team wants to serve multiple model versions simultaneously on a Databricks Model Serving endpoint. They configure the endpoint to send 80% of traffic to version 3 and 20% to version 4. After two weeks, version 4 shows higher accuracy. What is the next step in this deployment strategy?

options.text: Delete version 3 from the Model Registry and redeploy the endpoint with only version 4
options.explanation: Deleting version 3 from the Model Registry eliminates the rollback option if issues emerge after full promotion.

options.text: Retrain version 3 with version 4's hyperparameters to make them equivalent
options.explanation: Retraining version 3 with version 4's hyperparameters is counterproductive — if version 4 is better, the goal is to promote version 4, not equalize them.

options.text: Create a new separate endpoint for version 4 and update DNS routing
options.explanation: Creating a new separate endpoint for version 4 requires clients to update their DNS/URL and adds infrastructure complexity with no benefit over adjusting the existing endpoint's traffic split.

options.text: Update the endpoint's traffic split to 100% version 4 and 0% version 3, then monitor before retiring version 3
options.explanation: The standard A/B rollout completion pattern is to gradually shift traffic to the winning version — setting version 4 to 100% and version 3 to 0% within the same endpoint — while keeping version 3 registered and reachable for quick rollback.

questions.stem: An ML team wants to score a batch of 100K new customer records using a model that was trained using the Databricks Feature Store, where features are stored in a table with 'customer_id' as the primary key. Which FeatureStoreClient call is correct for batch scoring?

options.text: fs.score_batch('models:/ChurnModel/Production', df_with_features)
options.explanation: If df_with_features already contains all features, you bypass the Feature Store's automatic lookup — and score_batch() is specifically designed to accept just entity keys, not a pre-joined feature DataFrame.

options.text: fs.predict('models:/ChurnModel/Production', df_with_customer_ids)
options.explanation: Fs.predict() is not a valid FeatureStoreClient method.

options.text: fs.score_batch('models:/ChurnModel/Production', df_with_customer_ids)
options.explanation: FeatureStoreClient.score_batch() only requires a DataFrame containing the primary key column(s) (customer_id) plus any request-time columns. It automatically retrieves the required features from the Feature Store using the model's logged feature lineage metadata.

options.text: mlflow.pyfunc.load_model('models:/ChurnModel/Production').predict(df_with_customer_ids)
options.explanation: Loading the model with mlflow.pyfunc.load_model() bypasses Feature Store metadata, so automatic feature lookup is unavailable — the input DataFrame would need to already contain all features manually joined.

questions.stem: A team is evaluating real-time model serving latency on Databricks. Select TWO factors that most directly increase inference latency on a Model Serving endpoint. (Select TWO)

options.text: Cold start time when the endpoint scales from zero to one instance after a period of inactivity
options.explanation: When an endpoint scales to zero and receives a new request, it must spin up a container, load the model, and initialize the environment — adding seconds of cold start latency to that first request.

options.text: The number of model versions registered in the MLflow Model Registry
options.explanation: The number of registry versions does not affect serving endpoint latency — the endpoint only loads the served version.

options.text: Heavy preprocessing or feature transformation logic executed inside the model's predict() method
options.explanation: Any preprocessing (e.g., tokenization, complex feature engineering, large dataframe transforms) inside the predict() method executes serially on every request, directly adding to p99 latency.

options.text: The number of unused experiments in the MLflow tracking server
options.explanation: Unused experiments in the tracking server have no impact on the serving endpoint.

questions.stem: An ML engineer deploys a Structured Streaming pipeline that uses foreachBatch to apply a fraud detection model to micro-batches. The model is loaded inside the batch function. After running for 24 hours, they notice executor memory pressure. What is the root cause and the fix?

options.text: The model grows in size over time; fix by periodically calling gc.collect() inside the batch function
options.explanation: Model weights don't grow dynamically.

options.text: Streaming checkpoints are storing model weights; fix by disabling checkpointing
options.explanation: Checkpointing stores query state metadata, not model weights.

options.text: foreachBatch does not support model inference; migrate to a DataFrame API approach
options.explanation: ForeachBatch fully supports model inference.

options.text: The model is reloaded into worker memory on every micro-batch, accumulating without being released; fix by loading the model once outside the streaming query and broadcasting it
options.explanation: When mlflow.pyfunc.load_model() is called inside the foreachBatch function, a new copy of the model is loaded into executor JVM/Python worker memory on every micro-batch invocation. If micro-batches arrive faster than the garbage collector can reclaim the old model objects, memory accumulates, causing pressure. The fix is to load the model once before the streaming query starts and broadcast it using SparkContext.broadcast(), so each executor gets one copy that persists across batches.

questions.stem: A batch inference job uses mlflow.pyfunc.spark_udf() and runs on a 10-node cluster. The job completes but 3 worker nodes have error logs showing 'ModuleNotFoundError: No module named lightgbm'. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: The LightGBM model was not logged correctly in MLflow
options.explanation: A logging issue with the model would cause a different error at model load time, not a ModuleNotFoundError at inference time on specific workers.

options.text: The model's MLflow flavor is incompatible with the Spark version
options.explanation: LightGBM and Spark version compatibility is a separate concern and would cause a different type of error, not a missing module error on only some workers.

options.text: The spark_udf() was called with env_manager='local' and lightgbm is not installed on those 3 worker nodes
options.explanation: Env_manager='local' uses the Python environment already installed on each worker. If lightgbm was not installed uniformly on all cluster nodes — a common situation with autoscaled clusters where new nodes may not have all packages — some workers will fail with ModuleNotFoundError.

options.text: The result_type parameter was set incorrectly for a LightGBM model
options.explanation: An incorrect result_type causes type errors or shape mismatches in the UDF output, not a ModuleNotFoundError.

questions.stem: An ML engineer converts a scikit-learn model to ONNX for edge deployment. After conversion, they observe that the ONNX model's predictions for some inputs differ slightly from the original sklearn model. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: ONNX conversion applies additional L2 regularization to the model weights
options.explanation: ONNX conversion does not modify model weights or add regularization — it is a functional conversion of the computation graph.

options.text: The ONNX Runtime version must exactly match the sklearn version to produce identical results
options.explanation: There is no requirement for version matching between sklearn and ONNX Runtime — the ONNX format is an interoperability standard.

options.text: ONNX does not support the model architecture and is silently approximating it
options.explanation: Sklearn-onnx (skl2onnx) fully supports standard sklearn estimators and performs exact functional conversion — it does not silently approximate.

options.text: Floating-point precision differences between sklearn's Python/numpy operations and ONNX Runtime's optimized operations
options.explanation: ONNX Runtime executes computation graphs using highly optimized kernels with different floating-point operation ordering than numpy, which introduces small numerical differences due to floating-point non-associativity. These are typically in the 1e-6 to 1e-4 range and are expected.

questions.stem:
A model is served via a Databricks Model Serving REST endpoint. A developer sends a prediction request using the following curl command:

```bash
curl -X POST https://<workspace>/serving-endpoints/MyModel/invocations \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer <token>' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"dataframe_records": [{"feature1": 1.5, "feature2": 0.3}]}'
```

The endpoint returns a 400 error. The model was logged with a signature requiring feature1 and feature2 as floats. What is the most likely issue?

options.text: The JSON payload format should use 'inputs' instead of 'dataframe_records'
options.explanation: Databricks Model Serving (backed by MLflow serving) supports two payload formats: 'dataframe_split' (with columns and data keys) and 'dataframe_records' (list of dicts). However, some MLflow model server versions expect 'inputs' as the outer key for tensor-based inputs. If the endpoint is configured for the 'inputs' format (common for non-pyfunc models or specific signature types), 'dataframe_records' would return a 400. The correct format for pyfunc models is typically {'dataframe_records': [...]} or {'dataframe_split': {'columns': [...], 'data': [...]}}.

options.text: The Authorization header format is incorrect for Databricks endpoints
options.explanation: 'Bearer <token>' is the correct Databricks auth format.

options.text: The feature1 value 1.5 exceeds the maximum allowed float value
options.explanation: There is no maximum float cap.

options.text: The endpoint name 'MyModel' cannot contain uppercase letters
options.explanation: Endpoint names can contain uppercase letters.

questions.stem: An ML team wants to detect drift in a categorical feature (e.g., 'product_category' with 15 possible values). Which statistical test is most appropriate?

options.text: Kolmogorov-Smirnov (KS) test
options.explanation: The Kolmogorov-Smirnov (KS) test measures differences between two continuous cumulative distribution functions — it is not appropriate for categorical data with discrete counts.

options.text: Chi-squared goodness-of-fit test
options.explanation: The chi-squared goodness-of-fit test compares the observed frequency distribution of categorical values in production against the expected distribution from training, making it the standard choice for categorical drift detection.

options.text: T-test for independent samples
options.explanation: The t-test for independent samples compares the means of two continuous numeric distributions — it cannot be applied to categorical frequency counts.

options.text: Mann-Whitney U test
options.explanation: The Mann-Whitney U test is a non-parametric test comparing ranks of continuous distributions between two groups, not categorical frequency distributions.

questions.stem: A production ML model's Population Stability Index (PSI) for a key feature is calculated as 0.28. How should the monitoring team interpret this value and what action should they take?

options.text: PSI < 0.1 means no shift; 0.1-0.25 means moderate shift; PSI > 0.25 means significant shift requiring investigation. PSI=0.28 signals significant shift — trigger model review and potential retraining.
options.explanation: The standard PSI interpretation thresholds are: PSI < 0.1 (no significant shift), 0.1-0.25 (moderate shift, monitor closely), PSI > 0.25 (significant shift, investigate and consider retraining). A PSI of 0.28 exceeds the 0.25 threshold, signaling significant distribution shift that warrants model review.

options.text: PSI is on a 0-1 scale; 0.28 means 28% of the feature distribution has shifted, which is within the acceptable 30% threshold.
options.explanation: PSI is not a percentage scale — it has no upper bound and values above 0.25 indicate significant shift regardless of whether 0.30 is also significant.

options.text: PSI > 0 always requires immediate model retraining regardless of magnitude.
options.explanation: Minor PSI values (< 0.1) do not require retraining.

options.text: PSI of 0.28 indicates 72% model accuracy on the shifted distribution.
options.explanation: PSI has no relationship to accuracy percentage.

questions.stem: A Databricks Lakehouse Monitoring job is configured on an inference table that stores model predictions and ground truth labels. What type of monitor should the team configure to track model quality metrics (precision, recall) over time?

options.text: TimeSeries monitor
options.explanation: A TimeSeries monitor is for tables with a time dimension tracking non-ML time-series data — it does not have built-in model quality metric computation.

options.text: InferenceLog monitor
options.explanation: Databricks Lakehouse Monitoring's InferenceLog monitor type is specifically designed for tables that contain model predictions alongside ground truth labels. It computes model quality metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, RMSE) over time windows.

options.text: Snapshot monitor
options.explanation: A Snapshot monitor computes statistics for a single point-in-time profile of a table rather than tracking model quality over rolling time windows.

options.text: Profile monitor
options.explanation: A Profile monitor generates statistical summaries of a table's columns but is not the specific type for inference log analysis with ground truth labels.

questions.stem: An ML team is designing an automated retraining pipeline that triggers when model performance degrades in production. Select TWO signals that would be appropriate triggers for automated retraining. (Select TWO)

options.text: The 7-day rolling F1 score on labeled production data drops below 0.80, compared to a baseline of 0.87 at deployment
options.explanation: A statistically significant and sustained drop in F1 score against ground truth labels is a direct indicator of model performance degradation — the clearest trigger for retraining.

options.text: The number of prediction requests to the serving endpoint exceeds 10,000 per hour
options.explanation: High request volume indicates traffic patterns and a scaling concern, not model degradation — increased load does not mean predictions have become less accurate.

options.text: The KS test p-value for a key numerical feature drops below 0.05 for three consecutive monitoring windows
options.explanation: Sustained input feature drift (KS p-value < 0.05) for three consecutive windows suggests the data generating process has changed enough that the current model is likely making suboptimal predictions on the new distribution.

options.text: A new version of the ML framework (e.g., scikit-learn) is released
options.explanation: A new framework release is a dependency management concern and has no bearing on whether the current deployed model's predictions have degraded.

questions.stem: A data scientist calls mlflow.active_run() inside a training function. Under which condition does this return None?

options.text: When called before the first training run has ever been started in the session
options.explanation: Mlflow.active_run() returns the currently active Run object if a run is open (via mlflow.start_run() or autolog), and returns None when no run is currently active — such as when called outside any run context or before any run has been started.

options.text: When mlflow.autolog() is enabled — autolog disables the active run context
options.explanation: Autolog opens a run automatically, so active_run() would return that run.

options.text: When the MLflow tracking server is not reachable
options.explanation: Network connectivity affects logging calls but active_run() reads local Python state, not the server.

options.text: When the function is called on a worker node rather than the driver
options.explanation: Active_run() operates on driver-side Python state; it is not typically called inside Spark worker functions.

questions.stem: A data scientist uses mlflow.evaluate() with a custom metric function. They define `def my_metric(eval_df, builtin_metrics): return MetricValue(aggregate_results={'my_score': 0.92})`. Which parameter of mlflow.evaluate() accepts this function?

options.text: extra_metrics=[make_metric(eval_fn=my_metric, greater_is_better=True)]
options.explanation: Mlflow.evaluate() accepts custom metric functions through the extra_metrics parameter, which takes a list of EvaluationMetric objects created with mlflow.models.make_metric(). make_metric() wraps the custom function and requires specifying greater_is_better to determine optimality direction.

options.text: custom_metrics=[my_metric]
options.explanation: Custom_metrics is not the correct parameter name for mlflow.evaluate().

options.text: metric_functions={'my_metric': my_metric}
options.explanation: No dict-based metric_functions parameter exists.

options.text: evaluators=[my_metric]
options.explanation: Evaluators specifies which evaluator backend to use (e.g., 'default', 'shap'), not custom metric functions.

questions.stem: An ML team wants to share an MLflow experiment between two Databricks workspaces in the same account. A data scientist in workspace B needs read access to runs created in workspace A. What is the correct approach in Databricks?

options.text: Export the experiment as a JSON file from workspace A and import it into workspace B
options.explanation: Exporting as JSON and importing is a one-time migration technique, not a live sharing mechanism — changes after the export are not visible in workspace B.

options.text: Change the tracking URI in workspace B to point to workspace A's tracking server
options.explanation: Changing the tracking URI to point to workspace A's server is not a supported cross-workspace access pattern — each workspace's MLflow server is scoped to that workspace unless using Unity Catalog.

options.text: Duplicate the runs by re-logging all metrics and artifacts in workspace B
options.explanation: Duplicating runs by re-logging data creates synchronization problems and doubles storage costs without providing a live shared view.

options.text: Move all experiments to a Unity Catalog-backed MLflow tracking server accessible to both workspaces
options.explanation: Databricks Unity Catalog provides a unified governance layer across workspaces. When MLflow experiments are stored in a Unity Catalog-enabled shared metastore, users in both workspaces can access the same experiments and runs with appropriate permissions.

questions.stem: An ML engineer runs nested runs for an ensemble model: a parent run for the ensemble and child runs for each base learner. They call mlflow.start_run(nested=True) for each child. After all child runs finish, where should the ensemble model artifact be logged?

options.text: To one of the child runs that performed best, so the best base learner is promoted
options.explanation: Logging the ensemble to a child run conflates the component (one base learner) with the aggregate result (the ensemble) — models loaded from a child run URI would only be the base learner, not the ensemble.

options.text: To the parent run, since the ensemble represents the combined outcome of all base learners
options.explanation: In the nested run pattern for ensembles, the parent run represents the combined outcome of all base learners. The final ensemble model should be logged to the parent run so the artifact URI and model registration are associated with the ensemble entity.

options.text: To a separate new run created after the nested runs complete
options.explanation: Creating a separate new run after the nested runs complete fragments the experiment — the ensemble has no connection to the base learner runs in the MLflow UI.

options.text: To all child runs simultaneously using mlflow.log_artifact() with a shared artifact path
options.explanation: is technically possible but violates the intent of the nesting structure and is not the standard pattern — the ensemble is the parent-level artifact.

questions.stem: A data scientist defines a HyperOpt search space with hp.quniform('batch_size', 32, 512, 32). What values can this expression produce?

options.text: Any integer between 32 and 512, uniformly sampled
options.explanation: Quniform is quantized, not arbitrary integers.

options.text: A float uniformly sampled from (32, 512) then quantized to the nearest integer
options.explanation: The output is quantized to multiples of q, not to the nearest integer.

options.text: Exactly 32 or 512 with equal probability
options.explanation: Only the endpoints are not guaranteed; all multiples of q are sampled with roughly equal probability.

options.text: Multiples of 32 between 32 and 512: [32, 64, 96, ..., 512]
options.explanation: Hp.quniform(label, low, high, q) samples from round(uniform(low, high) / q) * q, producing values that are multiples of q within the [low, high] range. With q=32, the possible values are 32, 64, 96, ..., 512 — exactly the common batch size choices that are powers of 2 or multiples of 32, which is a practical hyperparameter constraint.

questions.stem: A data scientist calls mlflow.log_artifact('/tmp/model_card.html', artifact_path='documentation') and mlflow.log_artifacts('/tmp/plots/', artifact_path='visualizations') within the same run. Select TWO true statements about where these files are stored in the artifact store. (Select TWO)

options.text: model_card.html will be stored at 'documentation/model_card.html' relative to the run's artifact root
options.explanation: Mlflow.log_artifact(local_path, artifact_path) stores the single file at '<artifact_path>/<filename>' — so model_card.html lands at 'documentation/model_card.html' in the artifact store.

options.text: All files inside /tmp/plots/ will be stored at 'visualizations/<filename>' relative to the run's artifact root
options.explanation: Mlflow.log_artifacts(local_dir, artifact_path) recursively uploads all files in the local directory, placing each at '<artifact_path>/<relative_file_path>' — so every file in /tmp/plots/ lands under 'visualizations/'.

options.text: log_artifacts() with artifact_path will ignore subdirectories inside /tmp/plots/
options.explanation: Log_artifacts() does preserve directory structure for subdirectories.

options.text: Both calls write to the same artifact root directory, overwriting each other if filenames clash
options.explanation: The artifact_path parameters 'documentation' and 'visualizations' are different namespaces, so they cannot overwrite each other.

questions.stem: A data scientist wants the default artifact storage location for new MLflow experiments to be a specific DBFS path. Which MLflow function sets this at experiment creation time?

options.text: mlflow.set_tracking_uri('dbfs:/mlflow/artifacts')
options.explanation: Mlflow.set_tracking_uri() sets where the MLflow tracking server is located (the backend store URL), not where artifacts are stored within an experiment.

options.text: mlflow.create_experiment('MyExp', artifact_location='dbfs:/mlflow/artifacts/MyExp')
options.explanation: Mlflow.create_experiment() accepts an optional artifact_location parameter that sets the default root URI where all run artifacts for that experiment are stored. This must be set at creation time and cannot be changed afterward.

options.text: mlflow.set_experiment('MyExp', artifact_location='dbfs:/mlflow/artifacts/MyExp')
options.explanation: Mlflow.set_experiment() sets the active experiment context by name but does not accept an artifact_location parameter.

options.text: mlflow.log_artifact('/dev/null', artifact_path='dbfs:/mlflow/artifacts/MyExp')
options.explanation: Mlflow.log_artifact() is for uploading a specific file to the current run's artifact store — it is not a way to configure the experiment's default storage location.

questions.stem: A data scientist uses mlflow.log_input() to log a training dataset with source='dbfs:/data/train.delta'. Later, an auditor queries the dataset inputs for model version 7 of 'RiskModel'. Which sequence of calls retrieves this information?

options.text: client.get_registered_model('RiskModel').dataset_inputs
options.explanation: Registered model objects (from client.get_registered_model()) do not have a dataset_inputs attribute — that information is on the run, not the model.

options.text: client.get_model_version('RiskModel', '7').dataset_inputs
options.explanation: Model version objects (from client.get_model_version()) also do not have a dataset_inputs attribute directly — you must navigate to the associated run.

options.text: version = client.get_model_version('RiskModel', '7'); run = client.get_run(version.run_id); run.inputs.dataset_inputs
options.explanation: Dataset inputs logged with mlflow.log_input() are attached to the training run as RunInputs. The retrieval sequence is: (1) get the model version to find its run_id, (2) get the run using that run_id, (3) access run.inputs.dataset_inputs.

options.text: mlflow.search_runs(filter_string="tags.dataset_source='dbfs:/data/train.delta'")
options.explanation: Mlflow.search_runs() with a tag filter would find runs but does not return structured DatasetInput objects — it also assumes the dataset was stored as a tag, not a logged input.

questions.stem: An ML engineer wants to log both training and validation metrics to a single MLflow run using a single function call. They have a dict {'train_loss': 0.12, 'val_loss': 0.18, 'val_acc': 0.94}. Which call logs all three at once?

options.text: mlflow.log_metric('train_loss', 0.12); mlflow.log_metric('val_loss', 0.18); mlflow.log_metric('val_acc', 0.94)
options.explanation: Making three separate mlflow.log_metric() calls works but is inefficient — each call is a separate HTTP request to the tracking server, adding unnecessary overhead.

options.text: mlflow.set_tags({'train_loss': 0.12, 'val_loss': 0.18, 'val_acc': 0.94})
options.explanation: Mlflow.set_tags() stores values as string tags, not numeric metrics — they cannot be compared numerically or plotted on the metrics chart.

options.text: mlflow.log_params({'train_loss': 0.12, 'val_loss': 0.18, 'val_acc': 0.94})
options.explanation: Mlflow.log_params() stores key-value pairs as hyperparameter strings in the params table — they would appear in the params panel, not in the metrics chart, and cannot be plotted over time.

options.text: mlflow.log_metrics({'train_loss': 0.12, 'val_loss': 0.18, 'val_acc': 0.94})
options.explanation: Mlflow.log_metrics() (plural) accepts a dict of metric_name → float_value pairs and logs all of them in a single API call, which is more efficient than individual calls.

questions.stem: An MLOps engineer needs to set up an automated notification to a Slack channel whenever a model version transitions to the Staging stage in the MLflow Model Registry. Which MLflow registry webhook event should they use?

options.text: MODEL_VERSION_CREATED
options.explanation: MODEL_VERSION_CREATED fires when a new version is first registered, before any stage assignment — it does not indicate a stage transition.

options.text: TRANSITION_REQUEST_CREATED
options.explanation: TRANSITION_REQUEST_CREATED fires when a transition is requested but not yet approved — useful for approval workflows, not post-transition Slack notifications.

options.text: MODEL_VERSION_TRANSITIONED_STAGE
options.explanation: MODEL_VERSION_TRANSITIONED_STAGE fires when a model version's stage is actually changed (e.g., to Staging or Production). This is the correct event for triggering post-transition notifications.

options.text: REGISTERED_MODEL_CREATED
options.explanation: REGISTERED_MODEL_CREATED fires when a new model name is first registered in the registry, not when an existing version transitions stages.

questions.stem: A data scientist logs a model with mlflow.sklearn.log_model(model, 'model', extra_pip_requirements=['shap==0.42.0', 'pandas>=1.3']). How are these extra requirements stored in the model artifact?

options.text: They replace the auto-generated conda.yaml entirely with only the specified packages
options.explanation: The conda.yaml is auto-generated with all detected packages plus extra ones.

options.text: They are appended to the model's requirements.txt file, which is used alongside conda.yaml at serving time
options.explanation: Extra_pip_requirements are appended to the model's requirements.txt file (not the conda.yaml). The requirements.txt is generated alongside conda.yaml in the model artifact directory. MLflow serving environments install both conda.yaml dependencies and requirements.txt dependencies. The extra_pip_requirements are additive — they do not replace the auto-detected dependencies.

options.text: They are stored as a run tag with key 'extra_pip_requirements'
options.explanation: Requirements are stored in the model artifact, not run tags.

options.text: They override the conda.yaml by replacing the pip section with only the extra requirements
options.explanation: Extra_pip_requirements are appended to requirements.txt, not used to override the conda.yaml pip section.

questions.stem: A Unity Catalog model 'prod.ml.ChurnPredictor' has version 5 tagged as 'champion'. A new version 6 is trained and passes validation. The team wants to atomically promote version 6 to champion and demote version 5 to challenger without a gap. Which set of calls achieves this?

options.text: client.delete_registered_model_alias('prod.ml.ChurnPredictor', 'champion'); client.set_registered_model_alias('prod.ml.ChurnPredictor', 'champion', 6)
options.explanation: Deleting the alias first creates a gap where no champion exists.

options.text: client.set_registered_model_alias('prod.ml.ChurnPredictor', 'champion', 6); client.set_registered_model_alias('prod.ml.ChurnPredictor', 'challenger', 5)
options.explanation: In the Unity Catalog registry, set_registered_model_alias() reassigns an alias to a new version. When you call set_registered_model_alias('...', 'champion', 6), the alias 'champion' is atomically moved from version 5 to version 6 — there is no gap because the alias always points to exactly one version. The second call assigns the 'challenger' alias to version 5.

options.text: client.transition_model_version_stage('prod.ml.ChurnPredictor', 5, 'Archived'); client.transition_model_version_stage('prod.ml.ChurnPredictor', 6, 'Production')
options.explanation: Transition_model_version_stage() is a classic registry method, not available in Unity Catalog (which uses aliases instead of stages).

options.text: client.update_model_version('prod.ml.ChurnPredictor', version=6, alias='champion')
options.explanation: Update_model_version() has no alias parameter.

questions.stem: A data science team is evaluating using Databricks Feature Store vs. manual feature engineering for a production ML pipeline. Select TWO advantages of using the Feature Store's log_model() and score_batch() workflow over manual Spark joins + spark_udf(). (Select TWO)

options.text: The model artifact records which feature tables it depends on, enabling automatic feature retrieval at inference time from just entity keys
options.explanation: FeatureStoreClient.log_model() records the feature lineage (which tables, which keys, which columns) as model metadata. At inference time, score_batch() uses this metadata to automatically fetch the correct features — the caller only provides entity primary keys.

options.text: Feature Store log_model() automatically performs hyperparameter tuning before serializing the model
options.explanation: Feature Store has no hyperparameter optimization capability.

options.text: Eliminates the need to write and maintain join logic between entity tables and feature tables in the inference pipeline
options.explanation: This lineage-driven auto-lookup eliminates manual join code in inference pipelines, reducing maintenance burden and preventing join logic drift between training and serving.

options.text: Feature Store models are automatically deployed to a Model Serving endpoint upon registration
options.explanation: Feature Store model registration does not automatically create a serving endpoint; that is a separate Databricks Model Serving action.

questions.stem: An ML engineer wants to search for all registered model versions of 'SalesModel' that are currently in the 'Production' stage. Which filter_string correctly retrieves them?

options.text: MlflowClient().search_model_versions("name='SalesModel' AND stage='Production'")
options.explanation: Search_model_versions() supports filtering by 'stage' as a filter attribute in the filter string, allowing server-side filtering. This is the most efficient approach for retrieving all Production versions of a specific model.

options.text: MlflowClient().get_latest_versions('SalesModel', stages=['Production'])
options.explanation: Get_latest_versions() returns only the single latest version per requested stage — if multiple versions are in Production simultaneously, earlier ones would be missed.

options.text: MlflowClient().search_model_versions("name='SalesModel'") and then filter client-side by version.current_stage == 'Production'
options.explanation: Retrieving all versions and filtering client-side works but is inefficient for large model registries — it fetches all versions across all stages before discarding non-Production ones.

options.text: MlflowClient().list_model_versions('SalesModel', stage='Production')
options.explanation: List_model_versions() with a stage parameter does not exist as a valid MlflowClient method.

questions.stem: A data scientist needs to programmatically retrieve the artifact URI of a specific model version so they can inspect the raw model files. Given version = client.get_model_version('MyModel', '3'), which attribute contains the artifact URI?

options.text: version.artifact_uri
options.explanation: Version.artifact_uri is not a standard attribute on the ModelVersion object — this field does not exist on the returned protobuf object.

options.text: version.source
options.explanation: The ModelVersion object returned by get_model_version() stores the artifact location in the 'source' attribute — this is the runs:/<run_id>/<artifact_path> URI pointing to the original logged model in the run's artifact store.

options.text: version.run_artifact_uri
options.explanation: Version.run_artifact_uri does not exist as an attribute on ModelVersion.

options.text: version.model_uri
options.explanation: Version.model_uri is not a ModelVersion attribute — the models:/ URI is constructed from the model name and version number by the caller, not stored as a field on the object.

questions.stem: An ML team uses Databricks Feature Store to build training datasets. They have a label DataFrame with columns ['user_id', 'churn_label', 'event_ts'] and a feature table 'catalog.features.user_activity' with primary key 'user_id' and timestamp key 'activity_ts'. Which FeatureLookup correctly performs point-in-time lookup using event_ts from the label DataFrame?

options.text: FeatureLookup(table_name='catalog.features.user_activity', feature_names=['clicks_7d', 'sessions_30d'], lookup_key='user_id')
options.explanation: It omits timestamp_lookup_key, disabling point-in-time correctness.

options.text: FeatureLookup(table_name='catalog.features.user_activity', lookup_key='user_id', point_in_time=True)
options.explanation: Point_in_time is not a valid FeatureLookup parameter.

options.text: FeatureLookup(table_name='catalog.features.user_activity', feature_names=['clicks_7d', 'sessions_30d'], lookup_key='user_id', timestamp_lookup_key='activity_ts')
options.explanation: 'activity_ts' is the feature table's own timestamp column, not the label DataFrame's event timestamp — timestamp_lookup_key must reference the observation time in the label data.

options.text: FeatureLookup(table_name='catalog.features.user_activity', feature_names=['clicks_7d', 'sessions_30d'], lookup_key='user_id', timestamp_lookup_key='event_ts')
options.explanation: Timestamp_lookup_key must reference the column in the label/observation DataFrame (not the feature table) that holds the event timestamp. In this case, 'event_ts' is the observation time in the label DataFrame, and the Feature Store will look up feature values from 'user_activity' as of each row's 'event_ts'.

questions.stem: After registering a model, an ML engineer uses mlflow.artifacts.download_artifacts(run_id='abc123', artifact_path='model') to retrieve the model files. What is the local directory structure that results from this call?

options.text: A single file named 'model' is downloaded to the current working directory
options.explanation: Models are directories, not single files.

options.text: The full artifact directory at 'model' path in run abc123 is downloaded, including MLmodel file, conda.yaml, python_env.yaml, and the model weights
options.explanation: Mlflow.artifacts.download_artifacts() with a run_id and artifact_path downloads the entire artifact subtree at that path. MLflow model artifacts are directories containing: the MLmodel manifest, conda.yaml, python_env.yaml, requirements.txt, and the serialized model weights (e.g., model.pkl for sklearn, model.pt for PyTorch). The entire directory is downloaded recursively.

options.text: Only the MLmodel manifest file is downloaded; model weights require a separate call
options.explanation: The full directory is downloaded, not just the manifest.

options.text: The download fails because artifact_path must be a full URI, not a relative path
options.explanation: Artifact_path is a relative path within the run's artifact store, which is a valid and required input format.

questions.stem: A model governance policy requires all Production models in the classic registry to have a non-None description AND a tag 'approved_by' set to a reviewer name. A data scientist attempts to promote a version without these. What is the programmatic enforcement pattern?

options.text: Configure a registry webhook that blocks the stage transition if governance checks fail
options.explanation: Registry webhooks fire asynchronously after events occur and cannot block synchronous stage transitions — they are notification/CI triggers, not access control gates.

options.text: Before calling transition_model_version_stage(), check version.description is not None and 'approved_by' in client.get_model_version('M', v).tags; raise an error if checks fail
options.explanation: Programmatic governance gates are implemented as pre-condition checks in code before calling transition_model_version_stage(). The standard pattern is: retrieve the version, check its description and tags, and only proceed if all checks pass.

options.text: Set a registry-level policy in the Databricks workspace settings that enforces tags before transitions
options.explanation: No workspace-level policy enforcement mechanism exists in MLflow for blocking transitions based on custom field requirements.

options.text: Use mlflow.validate_model_version() to automatically enforce governance policies
options.explanation: Mlflow.validate_model_version() is not a valid MLflow function — governance validation must be implemented explicitly in application code.

questions.stem: An ML team wants to deploy a new version of their model to Databricks Model Serving with zero downtime. They currently have version 3 serving 100% of traffic. What is the recommended deployment sequence?

options.text: Take the endpoint offline, update it to serve version 4, then bring it back online
options.explanation: Taking the endpoint offline to update it causes downtime during the maintenance window, violating the zero-downtime requirement.

options.text: Create a new endpoint for version 4 and migrate clients by updating their base URL
options.explanation: Creating a new separate endpoint requires all clients to update their base URLs, which adds coordination complexity and is not zero-downtime from the client perspective.

options.text: Delete version 3 from the registry and register version 4 with the same version number
options.explanation: Version numbers in MLflow are immutable sequential integers — you cannot delete a version and register a new model with the same version number.

options.text: Configure the endpoint to serve both version 3 and version 4 simultaneously with a traffic split (e.g., 90%/10%), then gradually shift to 100% version 4
options.explanation: Databricks Model Serving supports in-place traffic splitting — adding a new model version to an existing endpoint with a portion of traffic allows zero-downtime deployment. The existing version continues serving while the new version is validated.

questions.stem: An ML engineer calls mlflow.deployments.get_deploy_client('databricks') and then uses client.create_endpoint() to deploy a model. What is the primary advantage of using the MLflow Deployments client over configuring the endpoint via the Databricks UI?

options.text: The MLflow Deployments client applies automatic traffic splitting between all registered model versions
options.explanation: Automatic traffic splitting is not a feature of the deployments client; traffic config is explicit.

options.text: Endpoint creation and configuration become code that can be version-controlled, code-reviewed, and automated in CI/CD pipelines
options.explanation: Using the MLflow Deployments API (mlflow.deployments.get_deploy_client) to create and configure endpoints makes infrastructure-as-code possible — endpoint configurations live in Python scripts or CI/CD pipelines, can be reviewed, tested, and reproduced across environments. This is the fundamental advantage of API-driven infrastructure over UI-based configuration.

options.text: The MLflow Deployments client uses a faster internal API that bypasses Databricks rate limits
options.explanation: The deployments client uses the same Databricks REST API as the UI.

options.text: The MLflow Deployments client automatically selects the optimal instance type for the model
options.explanation: Instance type selection is explicit in the endpoint configuration.

questions.stem: An ML engineer is deciding whether to use GPU instances for a Databricks Model Serving endpoint. The model is a 100-parameter logistic regression. Which statement best describes when GPU instances are cost-effective for Model Serving?

options.text: GPU instances are always faster than CPU and should be used for any production model
options.explanation: For small models, CPU is faster due to lower overhead.

options.text: GPU instances are cost-effective for large deep learning models (millions+ of parameters, e.g., transformer-based NLP or image models) where GPU parallelism provides substantial throughput gains over CPU
options.explanation: GPU acceleration provides the greatest benefit for large deep learning models with many matrix multiplications that can be parallelized on GPU cores. For a 100-parameter logistic regression, the computation is trivially fast on CPU — GPU overhead (data transfer, kernel launch) would actually add latency at higher cost. GPUs are cost-effective for transformer models, large neural networks, and image models where batch matrix operations saturate GPU compute.

options.text: GPU instances are required for any model trained using PyTorch or TensorFlow
options.explanation: PyTorch/TensorFlow models can serve efficiently on CPU if they are small.

options.text: GPU instances should be used when the serving endpoint has more than 100 concurrent requests per second
options.explanation: High concurrency benefits from auto-scaling replicas, not necessarily GPU.

questions.stem: An ML engineer wants to perform shadow deployment: the production model (v5) handles all live traffic and returns responses, while a shadow model (v6) receives a copy of each request but its predictions are discarded. What is the purpose of shadow deployment?

options.text: To reduce serving costs by sharing compute between v5 and v6
options.explanation: Shadow deployment actually increases cost because both models process each request.

options.text: To automatically switch to v6 if v5 exceeds its latency SLA
options.explanation: Shadow deployment does not implement automatic failover.

options.text: To allow v6 to learn from v5's predictions via online learning
options.explanation: Shadow deployment does not involve online learning.

options.text: To test v6 under real production traffic patterns — including edge cases and load levels — without any risk of affecting production outputs
options.explanation: Shadow deployment (also called shadow mode or dark launch) is specifically designed to evaluate a new model version against real production traffic without affecting production responses. v6 receives the same input as v5, generates predictions, and those predictions are logged for offline analysis. This allows the team to compare v6's behavior against real-world data distributions and edge cases that may not be present in test sets.

questions.stem: An ML engineer is implementing a pyfunc PythonModel that wraps a text preprocessing step (tokenization) and a BERT classification model. Select TWO best practices for the predict() method implementation. (Select TWO)

options.text: Load the BERT model and tokenizer in predict() on each call to ensure the latest weights are used
options.explanation: Loading BERT in predict() on every call would add seconds of latency per request and repeatedly allocate GPU memory.

options.text: Load the BERT model and tokenizer in load_context() once at model load time, storing them as instance attributes
options.explanation: Load_context() is called once when the model is loaded for serving, making it the correct place to initialize expensive objects like BERT models and tokenizers. Storing them as self.bert_model and self.tokenizer avoids re-initialization on every request.

options.text: Accept both pandas DataFrame and numpy array inputs in predict() by checking isinstance(model_input, pd.DataFrame)
options.explanation: Production pyfunc models receive inputs in different formats depending on the calling context (pandas from Spark UDF, dict from REST endpoint) — defensive type checking enables the model to handle both.

options.text: Return predictions as a plain Python list to maximize compatibility with downstream consumers
options.explanation: Returning a pandas Series or DataFrame is preferred for type safety, indexing, and downstream compatibility with spark_udf result_type expectations.

questions.stem: A Databricks Model Serving endpoint receives 10,000 requests per minute at peak. The endpoint is configured with provisioned concurrency of 10. Each request takes ~50ms. How many requests will queue (not be immediately served) at peak load?

options.text: None — the endpoint auto-scales instantly to handle any load
options.explanation: Auto-scaling is not instant; provisioned concurrency is what handles immediate load.

options.text: Approximately 10,000 * 0.050/60 = 8.3 concurrent requests needed; with concurrency 10, all requests are served with headroom
options.explanation: is correct. At 10,000 requests per minute with 50ms per request, the required concurrency is (10,000 req/min) × (50ms/req) × (1 min/60,000ms) ≈ 8.3 concurrent requests. With provisioned concurrency of 10, the endpoint has sufficient capacity (10 > 8.3) to handle peak load without queuing, leaving ~1.7 slots of headroom.

options.text: All 10,000 requests queue because provisioned concurrency only handles one request at a time
options.explanation: Provisioned concurrency of 10 means 10 simultaneous requests, not one at a time.

options.text: 10,000 - 10 = 9,990 requests queue because only 10 can be served simultaneously
options.explanation: The queue depth is not simply total_requests minus concurrency; it depends on arrival rate vs. service rate.

questions.stem: A data science team builds a custom monitoring solution using Evidently AI to generate data drift reports. Compared to Databricks Lakehouse Monitoring, what is the primary tradeoff of this approach?

options.text: Evidently AI reports are less accurate than Lakehouse Monitoring statistical tests
options.explanation: Evidently AI's statistical tests are rigorous and often more customizable.

options.text: Custom Evidently AI monitoring requires the team to build and maintain orchestration, storage, and alerting infrastructure that Lakehouse Monitoring provides out-of-the-box
options.explanation: The primary tradeoff of custom monitoring frameworks like Evidently AI vs. managed solutions like Databricks Lakehouse Monitoring is the infrastructure burden. Evidently AI provides rich drift reports and visualizations, but the team must integrate it with a job scheduler, a data store for drift metrics, a dashboard tool, and an alerting system. Lakehouse Monitoring provides a fully managed end-to-end pipeline with Delta table outputs, scheduled refresh, and built-in integration with Databricks SQL dashboards.

options.text: Evidently AI does not support numerical feature drift detection
options.explanation: Evidently AI supports numerical, categorical, and text drift.

options.text: Evidently AI reports cannot be scheduled and must be run manually
options.explanation: Evidently AI can be scheduled via any orchestrator.

questions.stem: A monitoring team runs a KS test on a revenue feature: from scipy.stats import ks_2samp; stat, p = ks_2samp(train_values, prod_values). The result is stat=0.04, p=0.62. How should this result be interpreted?

options.text: The large KS statistic of 0.04 relative to the p-value indicates significant drift
options.explanation: 0.04 is a small KS statistic (max possible is 1.0); the interpretation must use the p-value.

options.text: The p-value of 0.62 is well above the 0.05 significance threshold, meaning we fail to reject the null hypothesis — there is no statistically significant evidence of drift
options.explanation: A p-value of 0.62 means that under the null hypothesis (the two samples come from the same distribution), there is a 62% probability of observing a KS statistic as large as 0.04 by chance alone. Since 0.62 >> 0.05 (the common significance level), we fail to reject the null hypothesis — the evidence does not support concluding there is distributional drift.

options.text: A p-value of 0.62 means 62% of the distribution has drifted
options.explanation: P-values are not percentages of drift.

options.text: The KS statistic of 0.04 confirms exactly 4% drift in the feature values
options.explanation: The KS statistic measures the maximum absolute difference between CDFs, not a percentage of feature values that drifted.

questions.stem: A production recommendation model has a 14-day ground truth latency (user purchases are recorded with a 2-week delay). The team wants to monitor model quality in near-real-time. Which approach best addresses this latency constraint?

options.text: Wait 14 days before computing any monitoring metrics — accuracy cannot be estimated without true labels
options.explanation: Waiting 14 days before any monitoring means silent degradation goes undetected for two weeks — proxy monitoring during the label latency window is valuable even without exact accuracy numbers.

options.text: Use prediction distribution monitoring as a leading indicator of model health, and compute true accuracy metrics on a 14-day rolling lag when labels become available
options.explanation: The standard approach for ground truth latency uses a two-layer strategy: leading indicators available immediately (prediction score drift, input feature drift, proxy metrics) plus lagged true quality metrics computed on the rolling labeled window.

options.text: Retrain the model every 14 days regardless of drift to ensure it is always current
options.explanation: Unconditional retraining every 14 days wastes compute when the model is performing well — retraining should be triggered by detected degradation, not a fixed calendar.

options.text: Switch to a simpler rule-based model that does not require monitoring
options.explanation: Replacing an ML model with rule-based logic to avoid monitoring complexity defeats the purpose — it eliminates the ML capability without actually solving the monitoring problem.

questions.stem: A team observes that their classification model's predicted positive rate has increased from 5% at training time to 22% in production. The input feature distributions appear stable. What type of drift is this, and what is the most likely explanation?

options.text: Data drift — the input features have shifted even though the KS test shows no significant change
options.explanation: Data drift refers to P(X) change — shifts in the input feature distribution. The scenario explicitly states that input feature distributions appear stable, ruling out data drift.

options.text: Prediction drift (output drift) — the model's predicted label distribution has shifted, potentially indicating the true positive rate in the population has genuinely increased, or concept drift has caused the model to misclassify
options.explanation: When input feature distributions are stable but the predicted label distribution shifts (5% → 22% positive rate), this is prediction drift or output drift. The cause may be genuine population change or concept drift.

options.text: Model version drift — a different model version was accidentally deployed
options.explanation: Model version drift would typically cause sudden discontinuous changes at a deployment event, not a gradual shift from 5% to 22% positive rate over time.

options.text: Label drift — the training labels were imbalanced, causing the model to over-predict positives
options.explanation: Label drift refers to changes in the training label distribution (a historical artifact), not the distribution of predictions in production.

questions.stem: A team uses Databricks Lakehouse Monitoring on an inference table with a TIMESERIES monitor. They notice the daily accuracy metric in the drift metrics table is NULL for several days. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: Accuracy cannot be computed for TIMESERIES monitors — use InferenceLog type instead
options.explanation: TIMESERIES monitors do compute accuracy if labels are available.

options.text: The ground truth label column contains NULL values for those days, making accuracy computation impossible
options.explanation: is partially plausible but NULLs in labels would typically result in metric computation on non-null rows, not complete suppression.

options.text: The monitoring job failed on those days and the metrics were not written
options.explanation: is possible but would affect all metrics, not just accuracy for specific windows.

options.text: The inference table had fewer rows than the minimum threshold for those time windows, causing Lakehouse Monitoring to suppress metrics to avoid statistically unreliable results
options.explanation: Databricks Lakehouse Monitoring applies a minimum row count threshold per time window before computing aggregate statistics and model quality metrics. If a particular daily window contains too few rows (e.g., during a low-traffic weekend or a data pipeline outage that delayed writes), the monitor suppresses metrics for that window rather than reporting unreliable statistics computed from very small samples.

questions.stem: An ML platform team is designing monitoring for a recommendation system. They cannot directly observe ground truth (whether users would have clicked a recommendation they didn't see). Select TWO proxy monitoring approaches appropriate for this case. (Select TWO)

options.text: Track the click-through rate (CTR) on recommended items as a business proxy metric for recommendation quality
options.explanation: CTR on recommended items is a real-time, observable business proxy metric that correlates with recommendation quality. A sustained decline in CTR is an actionable signal of model degradation even without explicit relevance labels.

options.text: Compute precision@k directly against future purchases as the ground truth, ignoring the causality problem
options.explanation: Computing precision@k against future purchases suffers from severe selection bias — users can only purchase items they were shown, so this metric is confounded by the model's own recommendations and cannot serve as unbiased ground truth.

options.text: Monitor the distribution of recommendation score outputs for drift — a significant shift in score distribution without input drift may indicate model degradation
options.explanation: Monitoring recommendation score distributions provides a statistical proxy — if the model starts assigning systematically different scores to similar item-user pairs (e.g., all scores collapsing near 0.5), this signals potential model issues without requiring ground truth labels.

options.text: Replace the ML model with a rule-based system to eliminate the ground truth latency problem
options.explanation: Replacing the ML model with a rule-based system to avoid the ground truth problem abandons ML monitoring rather than solving it — it does not address the underlying challenge.
